one_line_takeaway: ProToken is a token-level, gradient-weighted, layer-restricted provenance/attribution technique that identifies which federated-learning client contributed to a given LLM-generated token or response, evaluated (not attacked or defended) using injected backdoor triggers purely as a ground-truth-labeling device -- it is an interpretability/debugging contribution, not a security defense or attack paper.
key_finding: ProToken achieves 98.62% average attribution accuracy across 16 configurations (4 models x 4 domains, 6-client baseline), with per-configuration range 40-100%, reaching 100% in several Gemma/SmolLM configurations and >92.5% for Llama and Qwen. Gradient-based relevance weighting (RQ2) is shown to be essential: with gradient weighting, mean per-layer attribution accuracy is 66.34% (range 55.0-83.33%) vs. 35.71% (range 22.94-56.20%) without it -- an average 1.86x improvement, consistent across all 16 configurations. Restricting attribution to only the last N transformer layers is both accurate and cheap (RQ3): ProToken reaches 100% attribution accuracy across all tested layer counts for all four models, and overhead scales roughly linearly with monitored layers -- for Gemma-3-270M-it (18 layers total), overhead ranges from 1.10s (3 layers) to 1.42s (all layers), a 29% increase, up to 1.87s for the deepest model tested. At 9.2x client scale (55 clients, 25 malicious), ProToken maintains 92.00% average attribution accuracy on Gemma and 95.24% on Qwen, with continued clear log-scale probability separation between responsible (clients 0-24) and non-responsible (clients 25-54) clients (Figure 7); for Gemma specifically, benign mean token accuracy rose from 61.89% to 73.27% (11.38 pp gain) over 15 rounds while backdoor accuracy rose from 35.86% to 79.61%, confirming the trigger pattern was learned without destroying benign performance.
core_contribution: ProToken is a token-level provenance attribution methodology for federated LLMs: given a prompt and a generated response from a federated global model G, it identifies which client(s) contributed to specific tokens/responses without accessing raw client data. It exploits (1) the linearity of FL aggregation (e.g., FedAvg) to decompose the global model's forward computation into a weighted sum of per-client layer-wise computations, (2) the observation that transformer models concentrate task-specific signal in later blocks (specifically the self-attention output projection and final MLP layer of the last N transformer blocks), restricting attribution to a small, tractable subset of layers, and (3) gradient-based relevance weighting -- computing the inner product of a client's hypothetical layer activation (client weights applied to the global model's layer input) with the gradient of the predicted token's logit w.r.t. that layer's global hidden state -- to filter out activations irrelevant to the specific token being generated. Per-token client scores are summed across generation steps and across selected layers, then normalized via softmax to produce a per-response client attribution distribution (Algorithms 1-2, Eqs. 1-11). The paper positions itself as the first token-level provenance/attribution method for federated LLMs, distinct from prior federated debugging work (Gill et al.'s FedDebug/FedDefender/TraceFL) which only handles classification models with discrete output spaces.
threat_model: Not applicable -- this is not a security paper. The paper's own stated problem is interpretability/accountability (client attribution for FL debugging, fair reward allocation, and trust verification), not a security defense. Backdoor injection is used purely as an evaluation instrument to manufacture verifiable ground-truth labels for attribution accuracy (a client's trigger-response pair provides an unambiguous 'this response came from client X' label), and the authors explicitly state: 'we stress that backdoor injections are employed solely for evaluation purposes, not for developing attack or defense strategies' and 'this backdoor-based evaluation serves solely as a proxy to assess ProToken's general client attribution capabilities.' The paper does not propose or evaluate a poisoning/backdoor attack, nor a defense against one; malicious-client identification is listed only as one of several downstream applications enabled by attribution (alongside debugging, reward allocation, and trust verification), not as the paper's central threat model.
limitations: The paper's own text surfaces several limitations, though it does not present a dedicated 'Limitations' section: (1) the evaluation's ground-truth construction relies entirely on injected backdoor triggers, meaning attribution accuracy is measured only on trigger-elicited, out-of-distribution sentinel responses rather than natural, non-backdoored generations -- the paper does not report accuracy on organic (non-backdoor) responses where ground truth is inherently ambiguous (the paper explicitly frames this ambiguity, i.e. distinguishing client-contributed knowledge from the base LLM's pre-training knowledge, as a 'fundamental challenge' it works around rather than solves). (2) Per-configuration accuracy varies widely (40-100%), and the paper does not analyze which configurations produced the lower end of that range or why. (3) The layer-selection heuristic (last N blocks, attention output projection + final MLP layer) is motivated by citations to prior interpretability literature (Peters et al. 2018; Tenney et al. 2019) rather than derived or proven for this specific attribution setting. (4) Models tested are small (270M-1B parameters); the paper does not evaluate larger, more capable LLMs. (5) Scalability testing (RQ4) is restricted to 2 of 4 model architectures (Gemma, Qwen) and a single domain (coding). (6) The method assumes white-box access to client model weights at attribution time (client models are injected into the global forward pass), which the paper does not discuss as a constraint on deployment.

datasets_used: MedAlpaca-derived medical instruction data (Han et al., 2023)
FinGPT-derived financial instruction data (Yang et al., 2023)
quantitative_results: 98.62% average attribution accuracy across 16 configurations (4 models x 4 domains), range 40-100%
Gradient weighting: 66.34% mean per-layer accuracy (range 55.0-83.33%) with gradients vs 35.71% (range 22.94-56.20%) without, a 1.86x improvement
100% attribution accuracy across all tested layer counts (3 layers to nearly all layers) for all four model architectures
Provenance overhead for Gemma-3-270M-it: 1.10s (3 layers) to 1.42s (all 18 layers), a 29% increase; up to 1.87s for the deepest model tested
At 55 clients (9.2x scale): 92.00% attribution accuracy on Gemma, 95.24% on Qwen
Gemma benign mean token accuracy: 61.89% at init to 73.27% at round 15 (11.38 pp gain); backdoor accuracy: 35.86% to 79.61%
baselines_compared: No direct experimental baseline method is compared head-to-head; the paper argues (in Related Work) that prior federated debugging/provenance techniques -- FedDebug, FedDefender, TraceFL (Gill et al. 2023a, 2023b, 2025) -- are inapplicable because they target classification models with discrete output spaces, not autoregressive LLM generation. The internal ablation baseline is ProToken with gradient weighting disabled (activation-magnitude-only attribution).
